Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

The image?  That's the first card in The Sacred Geometry Oracle, which is hot off the press and on its way to stores and warehouses as I write these words. Card 1, The Unmarked Card, when upright indicates that the situation is wide open and you can make of it what you will; when reversed, it means that you don't have any way of knowing what's actually going on and you may want to back off and wait for more information before proceeding. The oak leaves on the sides of the image tell you that this card belongs to the first third of the oracle, which corresponds to Calas, the principle of solidity and materiality. 

[IMAGE:1]

 I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain other gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

Good evening, and thanks as always for your time and energy here.

I got the opportunity to answer some of my questions on your post announcing The Way of the Golden Section, so I've a got a few fewer questions today:

1) With the Winter Solstice coming up, I'm wondering about a specific intersection of your oft-repeated advice that magic and religion are not the same thing: are seasonal rituals more important for religion, for magic, or equally important to each for different reasons? If the answer is both, what distinct things are such rituals doing for each approach?
2) I have worked my way up to the full SOP and will shortly embark on using it practically. When I do so, does the practical SOP take the place of the daily "standard" SOP, or should I do both? For example, if I'm using the sample given in the book and trying to invoke desired personal qualities and banish undesirable ones, should I do a standard SOP in the morning and the personal qualities one later in the day, or just the personal qualities one?
3) I've started Sane Occultism recently, and I know Dion Fortune has heavily influenced your views on the occult, so I'm curious: in what ways would you say your own views differ most strongly from hers?

Thanks much,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It depends on the religion, and on the system of magic! "Religion" and "magic" are both very broad categories. The intentions and effects of a Christian mass on Christmas are not the same as those of a Druid ceremony on the winter solstice. In the same way, the intentions and effects of a working of La Chose on the spring equinox by a lodge of Elus Coens are not the same as those of a Golden Dawn lodge celebrating the spring equinox on the same day.

2) Experiment and see what works best for you.

3) Her attitudes toward sexuality are very strongly shaped by her Christian faith and the attitudes of her time, and I think she was seriously mistaken about some details.

comments.title: Discordian SOP
comments.content:
Hi JMG-
Thanks for doing these. Do you know how one would do the Discordian version of the Sphere of Protection?

replies.title: Re: Discordian SOP
replies.content:
Yes, and in fact I've done it and taught it. You make the initial gestures with the words "Hail Eris," "All Hail Discordia," and "Kallisti." Then it gets weird.

Instead of six points in a nice orderly arrangement around you, there are five, and you have to figure out clairvoyantly where they are, as they move around at random. (One of them may be under the couch, for example.) The symbols you trace for each point is the outline of a golden apple. The holy words you invoke at each of them are of course the names of the five Discordian elements, chanted five times each: "Prickle-prickle-prickle-prickle-prickle," "Boom-boom-boom-boom-boom," and so on.

When you've found and invoked each of the five elemental symbols, stand in the center of the space and visualize a golden apple surrounding you, causing every hostile force to get confused and wander off in a daze.

(Ahem. Yes, it's very silly. The remarkable thing is that it works quite well in practice.)

replies.title: Re: Discordian SOP
replies.content:
That’s silly, all right. Do you have to believe in Eris, et al, for it to work?

—Princess Cutekitten

Belief isn't a concept that fits well in the world of Discordianism. To quote the Principia Discordia:

"GP: Is Eris true?

M2: I don't know man, I didn't do it."

And of course there's always the fifth of the Five Commandments of Discordianism, the Pentabarf:

"V - A Discordian is Prohibited of Believing what he reads."

(On the off chance you haven't yet, you can read the Principia Discordia online here.)

replies.title: Re: Discordian SOP
replies.content:
Oh my.

Back in 1968 or so, a group of merry pranksters founded the Discordian Society, which was either a serious metaphysical project disguised as a prank or a prank disguised as a serious metaphysical project. It's still a going concern. You can get a decent general summary here, and read the Principia Discordia, the (ahem) "sacred scripture" of the movement, here.

As for chaos magic, the original founders of chaos magic got the idea from the Illuminatus! trilogy by Robert Shea and Robert Anton Wilson, which is so full of Discordianism many Discordians treat it as a kind of New Testament. So the Discordians were chaos mages before there were chaos mages.

Since we're over halfway through MM, I suppose I'll post a few additional thoughts of my own. While the anonymous poster's comment last week originally was slightly disconcerting for me, the more that I've thought it through, the more trivial it actually seems. Santa Muerte works with most anyone, for reasons and goals that run the spectrum of good and bad. That seemed a little off-putting at first, but then I consider Scriptures like Matthew 5:45, where Jesus tells his followers that God sends the sun and the rain on both the righteous and the unrighteous. Or the simple fact that when warring Christian nations fought in centuries past, they all prayed to the same God for success. Or what they thought was the same God, at any rate. Death is a more ambivalent and yet all encompassing force, so it seems natural that a Goddess of death could be approached for many different kinds of requests. It doesn't seem all that strange that Santa Muerte will protect a cartel leader and a simple Mexican shopkeeper at the same time.

Does that sound nonsensical to you, JMG?

replies.content:
This is entirely my own unverified gnosis, and my only source of authority is that I have been praying to Santisima for years, so take it with a complete grain of salt.

I feel that she truly, genuinely, and deeply loves (in the most holy sense) what it means to be human, and has a profoundly vested interest in the human experience. This encompasses everything from dancing and laughter to black rage to wailing grief. She is also strongly associated with oppressed populations (in particular LGB, women, and the very poor), and sometimes the only recourse of those without material power is what we might call black magic.

If we also take a look at non-Christian, pre-Christian, or non-Western belief systems, most have beings that humans gladly worship that are outside the binary of "good and evil". Some examples are Baron Cimitiere of Vodoun, Odin who delights in human sacrifice, savage and animalistic Ares of the battlefield, Kali Ma and her still existing animal sacrifice and blood drinking, or Pomba Jira of Afro-Brazilian traditions, etc, etc, etc.

Santa Muerte is downright tame compared to some other beings we've managed to make friends with!

replies.title: Re: SOP
replies.content:
1) Turn off the computer before you practice -- don't just let it go into sleep mode. (It's a good idea to do that anyway at least once a day so it can clear away temp files and the like.)

2) It's very awkward to practice magic around people who aren't sympathetic to that practice. comments.title: Planes of consciousness
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Would you be able to define the term "higher planes of consciousness". Its often used as a description of what (insert pop spirituality of your choice here) will let you access. It's usually thrown around with little definition and I saw it questioned on that which made me wonder if it could be defined more precisely.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Planes of consciousness
replies.content:
Sure. The basic concept is that human consciousness can operate on various levels. Most of us know what it feels like to have "brain fog" and not be able to think clearly; many of us also know what it's like to be in one of those moments where thoughts flow freely and everything clicks together. Those are examples of different planes of consciousness. The "higher" (in metaphorical terms) the plane of consciousness, the clearer your thinking is, the more easily you can understand things, and crucially, the easier it is for you to reflect on your own thinking and see flaws in it. The "lower" (again, this is a metaphor) your plane of consciousness, the more rigid and sluggish your thought processes become and the less easy it is to notice when you're thinking, saying, or doing something dumb.

That familiar reality has been wrenched into various unhelpful shapes by pop spirituality, and confused with things like clairvoyance and astral projection. Operating on a higher plane of consciousness won't make you clairvoyant -- though it may keep you from making a fool of yourself in responding to a clairvoyant experience!

replies.title: Re: Planes of consciousness
replies.content: Great thanks. That definition makes a lot of sense and as a bonus it would even make sense to materialists!

comments.title: Astrology edge cases
comments.content:
I have a question about birth charts (it probably applies to any chart): I know a 30 minute period in which I was born. I don't think I can get more accurate information. At the start of the period, the Ascendant is just barely in one sign, and at the end of the period it is just barely in the other sign. One is Aquarius, the other Capricorn, so the meanings would be quite different. This also affects some other parts of the chart, like the Descendant, Midheaven and so on.

What is the usual way to deal with this problem? Would I just read the charts from both ends of the time period and pick the one that seems to fit better? Do they average out? Or is there some general rule about edge cases, that when something is right on the edge of two areas in the chart, it counts for both, or always for the clockwise one, or something else?

replies.title: Re: Astrology edge cases
replies.content: You could try to find out which ascendent (Capricornus or Aquarius) do fit better to the part of your personality which the Ascendent denotes. There is a whole branch of natal astrology concerned with rectifying birth charts, if the birth time is unknown or poorly known.

replies.title: Re: Astrology edge cases
replies.content: As u/booklover1973 says, rectification is the art of narrowing down an uncertain birth time. It's best done by a skilled astrologer; if you can find someone competent, they can usually sort things out promptly.

replies.title: Re: Astrology edge cases
replies.content:
There is a procedure in William Lilly, I think it's on p. 501, which he calls Trutine or Scrutiny of Hermes, for rectifying a birth chart. If I understand the procedure, it goes something like this:

1) Use the approximate time of the birth (e.g., morning or evening or night) to determine the Moon sign;
2) Count thirty-nine weeks earlier than that to find the date of conception (or use the date of conception from the parents... they might know it ... ahem ... intimately).
3) Make the chart for the day of conception, and put the Ascendant in the same sign that is the *Sign of the Moon in the birth chart*;
3a) There's a fudge factor here: it can be the sign of the Moon, or the sign opposite the Moon (if the person knows, for example, that they were born in the daytime or at night time... this allows for roughly 12 hours of difference to get the right time of day if it's known);
4) The sign that the Moon is in, in the conception chart, is the sign of the Ascendant in the birth chart.
5) The degree (and minute) of the Moon in the conception chart, is the degree and minute of the Ascendant in the birth chart.

Now... I have run this procedure for the charts of five or six of my friends with known birth times, and found that it accurately predicts the birth degree within about three degrees — that is, it's good for people with middle-of-sign births, but not people who are born very close to the change-over from one Ascendant sign to another. The Moon moves just a little more than 12° 53', or 12.88°, or 773' (minutes) per day — or about 32-33' minutes, half a degree, per hour. So, roughly 2/3s of the days in any month, the Moon will be in a particular sign, and only that particular sign on the day of your birth... there's a lot of wiggle room there. But when you make the Moon sign of your birth chart into the Ascendant of your conception sign, there's considerably less wiggle room, and you may be able to narrow your chart down to a specific 2-4° range using this method.

I have asked Christopher Warnock and some other astrologers about this method, with mixed results. The more traditional astrologers regard this as guesswork and adding additional layers of fudge-factors and wobble room to an already imprecise process when you have an accurate birth time. Others feel that it's nice to be in the right range of time, even if you can't get it exactly; and that it's nice to have a historically attested process to follow even if you know it yields only guesswork. And it's worth saying that I now have guidance from some astrologers and astrological enthusiasts that anything involving in vitro fertilization, or premature births, yield wildly inaccurate results.

William Lilly himself says that the method is better than chance and worse than an accurate birth time. This is what I've found in the (two) consultations I've done using this method.

I hope this helps.

Under stars,
Andrew Watt

replies.content: Interesting. Yes, that's certainly an option.

A couple titles that I have found helpful:

1) Dreams: A Portal to the Source by Edward Whitmont and Sylvia Perera - this was written by Jungian psychologists and draws heavily on his whole approach (which I view as a plus), but more easily organized and presented for practical application. comments.title: Ship Captains & Divination; also the Opening and Closing Ritual
comments.content:
Once upon a time there was a captain of a great ship, set to sail across a wide and mysterious sea. He knew that a storm lurked over the horizon, and he was going to have to deal with it one way or another. First he consulted his meteorologists, asking for the best way to handle the storm. They said because of the wind patterns, it would be best to go around the left side of the storm. Next he consulted his oceanographers. They said because of the strong ocean currents, it was best to go around the right side of the storm. Finally, he consulted his engineers. They said the ship could was plenty strong and seaworthy, and they recommenced sailing straight on through the storm.

Now the captain, being a True Captain, was responsible for the ship, the crew, and the voyage. He was the one who ultimately bore the responsibility for the decision. As part of this, he was responsible for gathering all relevant information, assessing it, and making the right call. The captain had a good reputation among his crew. They trusted whatever decision he made, even if their opinion differed from his.

For this voyage, he decided to sail straight on through. The meteorologists and oceanographers didn't change their opinions, but they accepted the decision because they knew the captain typically made good decisions, that he had the "big picture", and that things would be ok in the end.

This story illustrates a question I have about divination. At this point in my journey, I ask the Tarot things like: "What will today be like?" or "What should I know about today?" However, if there is something important I'm facing (should I do X, Y, or Z?) I don't ask the Tarot. If it's important, then chances are good that I've already given it much thought. I've already analyzed it and I've already made a tentative decision. I am uncomfortable with asking the question, risking the oracle's answer differing from my plans, and then pushing on with my original plans anyway. And in this context, by "uncomfortable" what I am getting at here is that I am uncomfortable due to risking my relationship with the oracle.

I recall a MM answer from a while ago addressing something similar, where you said (as best I can remember, it's been quite a while) something like [paraphrasing]"....if you often go to a friend for advice, but you don't follow their advice, how do you think that makes them feel? Are they going to keep giving you good advice?"

I would prefer to be the captain of my own ship, and in this metaphor the oracle would be a source of information rather than the decision maker. I can see the value, however, in using divination results in the absence of analysis and information, or using it as a tie-breaker.

Questions:

1) Do you have any advice on how to approach divination in general, or the Tarot in particular, with weighty questions where the answer may conflict with one's judgement and analysis? Is my avoidance of asking these questions warranted? Or is it something one work's up to with ever developing skill?

1a) I gather from previous MM's that different oracles have different "personalities" and that the Tarot may be more sensitive to not being followed. What about Geomancy's personality in this regard? (I think that'll be my next divination system to learn.)

And now for something completely different...

2) "....must sprinkle with the lustral waters of the loud-resounding Sea." I've started the Opening and Closing Rituals from Circles of Power. When in the ritual, I hear the subtle "splat, splat, splat" of the lustral waters splashing against my bookshelf and walls. Though I haven't noticed it yet, I'm afraid of putting water spots on my books, walls, etc. I've started to dip the tiniest tip of my finger into the water, to get the tiniest bit or water to sprinkle. Do water spots on the walls become a problem for practitioners of the GD? Am I short changing my efforts by sprinkling too little water?

Finally I certainly echo other's thanks for your time and energy in hosting Magic Mondays. I get much out of them and find them hugely valuable!

replies.title: Re: Ship Captains & Divination; also the Opening and Closing Ritual
replies.content:
1) It's always wisest to be sure of your skill at divination before you rely on it too much for big important issues. Work up to it a bit at a time, and always consider divination one of the ingredients in a decision, not the only one that matters.

1a) Geomancy's not easily ruffled by anything. It also tends to be very frank, even when you don't want it to be.

2) I've done that for decades and never noticed a water spot.

The commentariat's thoughts about practices and beliefs is also welcome.

replies.title: Re: Advice on grieving and loss
replies.content:
First, please accept my condolences, and pass them on to your wife. That's a very hard thing to go through.

Second, traditional lore in every culture I know of has it that the recently dead not only hear prayers but benefit from them. They have a lot of work to do in the early stages of the afterlife, and they may or may not be able to respond in a way that the living can perceive, but prayers for the dead are like letters from home to someone who's gone on a long journey; they're appreciated, and helpful.

The book I recommend from the magical tradition is Dion Fortune's Through the Gates of Death, which discusses these matters in some detail.

As for myself, well, I have the advantage of knowing that death is a temporary separation rather than a permanent loss, so it's very much a matter of wishing someone bon voyage and then missing them, but knowing that we'll meet again.

replies.title: Re: Advice on grieving and loss
replies.content: Thank you, Mr. Greer. Your thoughts have been helpful to my wife.

replies.title: Re: Advice on grieving and loss
replies.content:
Zarvoc, I am sorry to hear of your loss.
You might want to check out the grief recovery handbook by john james and russel friedman.

comments.title: Guidance on Possibly Harmful Deities
comments.content:
Dear J.M. Greer,
Thank you for releasing The Way of the Golden Section! I'm on my third day of the training, and I'm looking forward to the books in the series still to come.

You've indicated that human beings begin to imitate the deities they worship. My concern is that working with certain deities, or inviting them in, could be dangerous or harmful. My particular concern is Jesus Christ. The family of traditions associated with his name has become a longstanding source of division, destructiveness, and conflict within my family, where there seems to be five or more different 'Jesii' involved. It seems the more passionately any of them believe they are following the correct way, the more intense such conflicts become.
No doubt the first place to turn to for guidance in these matters is the gods themselves. But since personal revelation seems to be so fallible, and only about as good as one's own personal filter and inner balance, it seems sensible to turn as well to a community and get sanity checks and guidance on such things.

1) When I think of the tragic history of his followers, and such quotes as 'Think not that I am come to send peace on earth: I came not to send peace, but a sword' and 'Father will be divided against son and son against father; mother against daughter and daughter against mother; and mother-in-law against daughter-in-law,' it seems really rather frightening. I just can't see how to interpret any of this in a positive light.
a) Does the danger seem to lie more within the religious or ideological constructs of the followers than with the deity himself (or deities themselves)?
b) If so, what are some of the safeguards that could be built into working with such a deity, or any deity, so as not to repeat these negative patterns? Given what has occurred within my family, I worry that any involvement with the Christianities could be highly unsafe and a gateway to the demonic realms.

2) I've meditated on why my own Egyptian deities don't seem to come with these problems, and came up with more or less the following: it seems that Egyptians were able to conceive of deities as being multiply embodied (a god in present in a temple in one place could have a different kind of embodiment in another), gods could embody each other, be each other's ba or participate in each other's embodiment, be multiple persons or manifestations of an underlying divinity, or be combined together; multiple creation accounts and mythologies were not mutually exclusive, and any of them could be used effectively in magical work; creeds or doctrines aren't important; and that experiencing one deity as an embodiment of divine unity ('the Only One') somehow didn't preclude an entirely different deity from being experienced as such by someone else. I know that doesn't mean every deity would want just any other within a given space, there seem to be patterns of harmony and some resonate well with others, others are discordant, some people are better attuned to one set than another, but that doesn't mean families must be torn apart, any more than one person's taste for classical music must lead to war with those who prefer rock & roll.
When I've turned to the gods for guidance on this matter, what they indicated to me is that the will is itself a potential divine faculty, or a basis of divinization, so its development cannot be short-circuited - to do so would conflict with the path of human beings toward divinization. So human beings can be allowed to fail, and since religions are simply human constructs to interface with the divine, they must be allowed to reflect the flaws of their human participants, according to their wills. Again, as a sanity check, does any of this seem to be on the right track?

3) If the source of this destructiveness lies within the human side of the religions, would it seem to be some flaws in the ideologies involved? I know that most of the various Christianities seem to agree that it's very important to have a set of unalterable doctrines one must believe in, but it seems they have never achieved a universal agreement on what those doctrines are.

4) Another possible source of these negative patterns has occurred to me: Analogous to socially parasitic creatures such as certain ants, like the slave-making or inquiline species here, https://www.nature.com/scitable/knowledge/library/social-parasitism-in-ants-13256421/
could there be beings that move into or take over a vulnerable religious egregor and feed on the support of its human adherents? If so, what could be some of those vulnerabilities, or some ways to prevent that sort of thing from happening? I can imagine this scenario also occurring, if it occurs, within magical orders or lodges.

Regards, and thank you for this forum,
Josh Rout

replies.title: Re: Guidance on Possibly Harmful Deities
replies.content:
1) We don't know much about gods, remember, and figuring out how much of the problem is due to the deity and how much to human constructs is difficult at best. The one thing that seems to work reliably is to look into versions of the faith that don't have the problems that concern you. Christianity has those -- the Quakers are among the examples that come to mind.

2) That seems quite reasonable.

3) It's probably not in the content of the ideologies. It may be in the very idea that having the right opinions is so very important.

4) That's a known phenomenon in magical lodges; Dion Fortune describes it as the problem of the tainted sphere. When any spiritually oriented group tolerates some form of abusive behavior, especially in its clergy or officers, the egregor of the entire organization can become contaminated, so that the abuses become entrenched. You can interpret this as the obsession of the egregor by a malign spirit. You prevent it by having strict rules and enforcing them; once it gets established, all you can do is close down the group, wait a while, and then start a new group with the evildoers excluded and with different symbols and rituals.

replies.title: Re: Guidance on Possibly Harmful Deities
replies.content: Thank you for the responses - much food for thought!

comments.title: Intentional community
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Recently you’ve written about the flight of fancy of people wanting to go and set-up their own community in order to make the world a better place. The problem being that they are usually running away to create a bubble which ultimately gets highjacked by ego and shadow. Yet there are communities that are successful and offer something back to society. I think you mentioned Findhorn once as an example. What do you think is the difference that makes it work? Is it a spiritual or magical component (although I imagine the latter creates as much problem as not). Would love your thoughts?

replies.title: Re: Intentional community
replies.content:
The crucial things you need to make an intentional community work are a secure and unquestioned leadership structure and a motivation to make people put up with poverty. Findhorn had those -- in the early days, Peter Caddy ran things, and if you didn't like that you were free to leave; and of course everyone's used to the idea that spiritual practice and material poverty go well together (as of course they do).

What dooms most intentional communities is that the people who found them want to keep up a middle class lifestyle, and don't want anyone to tell them what to do.

replies.content: Hmm...is this perhaps why the only successful intentional communities that I'm aware of tend to monastic ones, like Catholic and Buddhist monks and nuns?  I suppose a secular, atheistic goal just doesn't lend itself nearly as well to this kind of thing.

replies.title: Re: Intentional community
replies.content: Exactly. The Shakers are my go-to example, since they didn't have a charismatic guru once Mother Ann died; what they had was an effective leadership structure and a deeply spiritual ethos, and they thrived.
